title: Lewy Body Dementia: Comprehensive Clinical Nursing Guide

sections.title: Learning Objectives
content: 1. Describe the four core clinical features of Lewy body dementia (LBD) including cognitive fluctuation, visual hallucinations, parkinsonism, and REM sleep behaviour disorder.
2. Apply the 1-year rule to distinguish Dementia with Lewy Bodies (DLB) from Parkinson's Disease Dementia (PDD).
3. Identify the CRITICAL medication contraindication in LBD: explain why antipsychotic neuroleptics carry a life-threatening risk and which medications are considered safer alternatives.
4. Develop a nursing care plan that addresses the triple safety challenge of LBD: fall prevention, medication safety, and aspiration risk.
5. Explain the role of REM sleep behaviour disorder as a prodromal feature of LBD and its implications for early diagnosis.
6. Implement evidence-based non-pharmacological interventions for managing visual hallucinations in LBD.
7. Educate family members on the unique clinical features, unpredictable course, and safety requirements of LBD compared to Alzheimer's disease.

subsections.title: Pathophysiology — Alpha-Synuclein and Lewy Bodies
subsections.content:
Lewy body dementia (LBD) is the second most common neurodegenerative dementia after Alzheimer's disease, accounting for 10–15% of all dementia cases. LBD is a spectrum disorder encompassing two clinical diagnoses: Dementia with Lewy Bodies (DLB) and Parkinson's Disease Dementia (PDD).

Neuropathology: LBD is characterized by the abnormal accumulation of alpha-synuclein protein within neurons, forming eosinophilic intraneuronal inclusions called Lewy bodies and Lewy neurites. Alpha-synuclein is normally involved in synaptic vesicle regulation; in LBD, it misfolds, aggregates, and becomes toxic to neurons. Lewy bodies are found in the cortex (causing dementia), brainstem (causing parkinsonism — affecting dopaminergic neurons of the substantia nigra), and autonomic nervous system (causing orthostatic hypotension, constipation, urinary dysfunction).

Cholinergic deficit in LBD is MORE PROFOUND than in Alzheimer's disease — the cortical cholinergic deficit explains why rivastigmine (AChEI) has particularly robust evidence in LBD.

LBD is a synucleinopathy (same pathological protein as Parkinson's disease and multiple system atrophy) — this places it in the Parkinson's disease spectrum neuropathologically, even when dementia is the presenting feature.

subsections.title: The 1-Year Rule — DLB vs Parkinson's Disease Dementia
subsections.content:
The single most important diagnostic distinction in the LBD spectrum:

Dementia with Lewy Bodies (DLB):
- Dementia is the PRIMARY presenting symptom
- Motor (parkinsonian) symptoms appear WITHIN 1 YEAR of dementia onset, OR dementia precedes motor symptoms
- TIMING: Dementia FIRST (or concurrent with motor onset within 12 months)

Parkinson's Disease Dementia (PDD):
- Motor symptoms (tremor, rigidity, bradykinesia) occur FIRST
- Dementia develops MORE THAN 1 YEAR AFTER Parkinson's disease diagnosis
- TIMING: Motor FIRST, then dementia >1 year later

Clinical implication: The 1-year rule is the standard used in research and clinical practice. Both conditions share the same underlying neuropathology (alpha-synuclein Lewy bodies) and the same critical medication contraindication (antipsychotic neuroleptics). The distinction affects prognosis (DLB often has a more rapid and unpredictable course than PDD) and drug metabolism (levodopa causes more psychiatric side effects in DLB than PDD).

Memory anchor: '1 year' — if motor symptoms arrive within 1 year of the dementia, it's DLB. If motor symptoms have been present for more than 1 year before dementia, it's PDD.

subsections.title: Core Clinical Features of Lewy Body Dementia
subsections.content:
Consortium on Dementia with Lewy Bodies (CDLB) Diagnostic Criteria (2017):

DIAGNOSIS requires dementia PLUS:
Probable DLB: 2 core features, OR 1 core + 1 or more indicative biomarker
Possible DLB: 1 core feature, OR 1 or more indicative biomarker

CORE CLINICAL FEATURES (4):

1. FLUCTUATING COGNITION with variations in attention and alertness:
- Most specific feature of DLB. Dramatic variation in cognitive ability and consciousness level — ranging from near-normal function to severe confusion — within hours or days.
- May appear 'delirious' without a medical cause. Unlike delirium, there is no acute precipitant; it is intrinsic to the disease.
- Episodes: patient may be unresponsive for minutes-hours, then return to near-baseline.
- Clinical challenge: appears as delirium → may trigger antipsychotic use → DANGEROUS in LBD.

2. RECURRENT VISUAL HALLUCINATIONS:
- Present in 60–80% of DLB patients.
- Typically WELL-FORMED, DETAILED, VIVID: people, animals, children. NOT vague shapes.
- Often NON-THREATENING initially — patient may find them interesting or describe them calmly.
- Patient often has INSIGHT early: 'I know these aren't real, but I keep seeing a cat.'
- Later in disease: may become threatening and distressing.
- Key differentiator from AD: visual hallucinations in early-mid LBD are characteristic; in AD they occur only in late stages.

3. REM SLEEP BEHAVIOUR DISORDER (RBD):
- Normally during REM sleep, the body is atonic (paralyzed). In RBD, this atonia fails — patients PHYSICALLY ACT OUT their dreams: punching, kicking, shouting, falling out of bed.
- RBD may begin DECADES before cognitive symptoms — it is the most important prodromal marker of LBD.
- Patient (or bed partner) reports: 'He punched me in his sleep' 'She fell out of bed yelling' 'He was fighting with someone in his dream.'
- Diagnosis: overnight polysomnography (sleep study); clinically diagnosed by history.
- RBD is present in >80% of confirmed LBD cases.

4. ONE OR MORE FEATURES OF PARKINSONISM:
- Spontaneous (not drug-induced) motor features
- Bradykinesia (slowness of movement)
- Rest tremor (pill-rolling: less prominent than idiopathic PD)
- Rigidity (cogwheel or lead-pipe)
- Postural instability (impaired balance)
- NOTE: Parkinsonism in LBD responds poorly to levodopa compared to idiopathic PD

SUPPORTIVE FEATURES:
- Severe neuroleptic sensitivity (see safety alerts)
- Postural instability, repeated falls
- Syncope or transient loss of consciousness
- Severe autonomic dysfunction: orthostatic hypotension, urinary incontinence, constipation
- Hypersomnia (excessive daytime sleepiness)
- Hyposmia (loss of smell)
- Depression
- Anxiety

subsections.title: CRITICAL: Antipsychotic Neuroleptic Sensitivity in LBD
subsections.content:
This is the MOST CLINICALLY IMPORTANT and MOST EXAMINED feature of LBD.

The Problem: Antipsychotic medications (BOTH typical and most atypical antipsychotics) can cause SEVERE, POTENTIALLY FATAL reactions in patients with LBD. This is called neuroleptic sensitivity or neuroleptic malignant-like syndrome in LBD.

Severity: Approximately 50% of LBD patients who receive standard antipsychotics experience severe reactions. This is not a rare side effect — it is a predictable, drug-class-related response.

What happens: Antipsychotics block dopamine D2 receptors in the already dopamine-deficient basal ganglia of LBD patients, causing:
- Severe worsening of parkinsonism (profound rigidity)
- Impaired consciousness (ranging from sedation to coma)
- High fever (hyperthermia)
- Autonomic instability (labile BP, tachycardia)
- Aspiration pneumonia (due to profound rigidity and loss of protective reflexes)
- Death — 2–3× increased mortality compared to LBD patients not given antipsychotics

CONTRAINDICATED antipsychotics in LBD:
- ALL first-generation (typical) antipsychotics: haloperidol, chlorpromazine, fluphenazine, loxapine
- Most second-generation (atypical) antipsychotics: olanzapine, risperidone, aripiprazole, ziprasidone, amisulpride

RELATIVELY SAFER options (lowest D2 receptor affinity):
- Quetiapine: LOWEST D2 binding affinity of all antipsychotics; preferred if antipsychotic is absolutely necessary. STILL carries risk — informed consent required.
- Clozapine: Also low D2 affinity; evidence in PD psychosis; requires weekly blood monitoring (agranulocytosis risk).

For HALLUCINATIONS in LBD:
- Non-pharmacological first (see nursing interventions)
- If pharmacological treatment needed: rivastigmine (AChEI) — may reduce hallucinations
- Pimavanserin (Nuplazid): FDA-approved for Parkinson's disease psychosis; serotonin inverse agonist (no D2 activity) — emerging evidence in LBD
- Quetiapine: lowest-risk antipsychotic option if absolutely necessary; lowest effective dose
- NEVER haloperidol or standard antipsychotics

subsections.title: Pharmacotherapy — Evidence-Based Treatment
subsections.content:
1. Rivastigmine (Exelon) — EVIDENCE-BASED for LBD:
- AChEI specifically studied in DLB — a randomized controlled trial (McKeith 2000) showed significant improvement in cognitive fluctuations, attention, and visual hallucinations.
- LBD has a more profound cholinergic deficit than AD — AChEI effect is robust.
- Transdermal patch preferred for compliance and GI tolerance.
- Nursing: rotate patch sites; remove old patch before applying new; do not cut patch; monitor GI symptoms, heart rate.

2. Levodopa for Parkinsonism:
- Used cautiously in DLB (better tolerated in PDD).
- May WORSEN visual hallucinations and psychiatric symptoms in DLB.
- Start at lowest possible dose; titrate slowly.
- If benefit for mobility without psychiatric worsening: continue.
- Never stop abruptly (risk of neuroleptic malignant syndrome-like reaction).

3. Clonazepam for REM Sleep Behaviour Disorder:
- First-line for RBD: 0.5 mg at bedtime; titrate cautiously.
- Reduces injury during RBD episodes.
- Monitor: sedation, fall risk (benzodiazepine in older adults — use carefully, lowest effective dose).
- Alternative: melatonin 3–12 mg at bedtime (emerging evidence, safer profile).

4. Management of Orthostatic Hypotension:
- Non-pharmacological: rise slowly, compression stockings, elevate head of bed 30°, salt/fluid intake, avoid large meals.
- Pharmacological: fludrocortisone (mineralocorticoid), midodrine (alpha-1 agonist).
- Avoid: antihypertensives at bedtime (worsen nocturnal hypotension).

5. Depression in LBD:
- SSRIs: use with caution (may worsen parkinsonism in some patients)
- Sertraline, citalopram: generally better tolerated
- AVOID: TCAs (anticholinergic burden, cardiac risk, fall risk)

sections.title: Normal Aging vs LBD Features
sections.content:
Normal aging does not cause:
- Vivid, detailed visual hallucinations
- Significant day-to-day fluctuation in consciousness level
- REM sleep behaviour disorder (acting out dreams physically)
- Parkinsonism (tremor, rigidity, bradykinesia)
- Orthostatic hypotension causing repeated falls

Normal aging DOES cause:
- Some slowing of processing speed
- Mild reduction in recall speed
- Some sleep architecture changes (more frequent awakenings, less deep sleep)
- Mild balance reduction

Key insight: When a family member reports 'He seems fine one day and barely knows where he is the next' — this is NOT normal aging. This dramatic fluctuation is pathological and characteristic of LBD until proven otherwise.

sections.title: Abnormal Findings and Red Flags
sections.content:
LBD-Specific Red Flags Requiring Urgent Action:

1. Antipsychotic prescription in a patient with known or suspected LBD:
- NEVER administer haloperidol, risperidone, olanzapine, or aripiprazole to a patient with LBD
- Medical emergency if these are prescribed: notify prescriber BEFORE administering

2. Sudden severe rigidity + fever + decreased consciousness in LBD patient:
- Suspect neuroleptic sensitivity reaction (even to quetiapine)
- OR levodopa-related complication
- Medical emergency: stop suspected agent, supportive care, ICU transfer

3. Fall with injury:
- LBD has BOTH parkinsonism (gait instability) AND cognitive fluctuation (sudden periods of reduced alertness) — this combination creates extreme fall risk
- Head injury (subdural hematoma) can precipitate acute-on-chronic cognitive deterioration

4. Sudden loss of consciousness / syncope:
- Autonomic dysfunction in LBD causes orthostatic hypotension → syncope → fall → head injury
- Cardiac monitoring may be required

5. New dysphagia:
- Parkinsonism impairs swallowing coordination — aspiration pneumonia is common cause of death in LBD
- Immediate SLP referral

sections.title: Assessment Tools and Clinical Approach
sections.content:
Cognitive Assessment:
- MoCA (preferred over MMSE): captures executive function and attention deficits characteristic of LBD
- The fluctuating cognition of LBD means test scores vary dramatically day-to-day — document date and patient's apparent alertness level at time of testing
- A patient may score 26 on a good day and 18 on a bad day — this fluctuation itself is diagnostic information

Motor Assessment:
- UPDRS (Unified Parkinson's Disease Rating Scale): motor examination component for quantifying parkinsonism
- Document: tremor (rest vs. action), rigidity (cogwheel), bradykinesia, gait (shuffling, freezing), posture (stooped), postural instability (pull test)

Sleep Assessment:
- RBD Screen Questionnaire: validated 10-item caregiver questionnaire for RBD symptoms
- Ask specifically: 'Does your partner move, kick, punch, or shout during sleep? Have they injured themselves or you during sleep?'

Autonomic Assessment:
- Orthostatic vital signs: lying → sitting → standing BP and HR at 1 minute and 3 minutes
- Orthostatic hypotension: systolic BP drop ≥20 mmHg or diastolic drop ≥10 mmHg
- Document: dizziness, near-syncope, falls related to position changes

Hallucination Assessment:
- Ask directly and non-judgmentally: 'Some people with this type of memory problem see things that others around them don't see. Has that happened to you?'
- Document: frequency, content (detailed people/animals vs. vague shapes), emotional response (calm vs. frightened), insight (knows it's not real vs. believes it's real), safety impact

Fall Risk Assessment:
- Timed Up and Go (TUG) test: >12 seconds = elevated fall risk
- Berg Balance Scale
- Environmental hazard assessment
- History of falls (number, circumstances, injuries)
- Orthostatic hypotension contribution

sections.title: Nursing Interventions
sections.content:
MEDICATION SAFETY (Priority #1):
- Every admission: identify LBD diagnosis in medication reconciliation
- Flag chart: 'ANTIPSYCHOTIC CONTRAINDICATED — LBD DIAGNOSIS'
- Never administer haloperidol PRN for behavioral management in LBD patients
- If antipsychotic is ordered by prescriber: question the order, clarify LBD diagnosis, document the concern, escalate if necessary
- Educate all team members (aides, PSWs, transport staff, on-call physicians) about this contraindication

FALL PREVENTION (Priority #2):
- Fall risk is extreme in LBD: parkinsonism + cognitive fluctuation + orthostatic hypotension = triple risk
- Environment: remove rugs, clear pathways, grab bars, handheld shower, non-skid footwear
- Bed: low position, call bell accessible, bed alarm if appropriate
- Orthostatism: stand slowly, supervise all position changes, recheck BP standing before ambulation
- Footwear: well-fitting, rubber-soled, closed-toe; no slippers without grip
- Assistive devices: walker or cane appropriate to gait pattern; physiotherapy referral
- Supervision: increased monitoring during cognitive fluctuation episodes — the patient who was alert 30 minutes ago may now be too confused to call for help

ASPIRATION PRECAUTIONS:
- Assess swallowing at every stage of care
- Position: seated upright (90°) for all meals and 30 minutes after
- Modified texture diet if dysphagia identified (SLP to specify)
- Chin tuck technique for thin liquids if appropriate
- Oral care: post-meal to prevent aspiration of food particles
- Signs of aspiration: coughing, gurgling voice, wet breath sounds, recurrent pneumonia

VISUAL HALLUCINATION MANAGEMENT (Non-Pharmacological):
- Do NOT argue that the hallucination is not real — this is distressing without therapeutic benefit
- Assess emotional response: 'How does seeing that make you feel?'
- If non-distressing: no intervention needed; acknowledge calmly
- If distressing: reassure the patient they are safe; redirect attention; increase lighting (dim lighting worsens visual hallucinations); check vision (glasses in, lighting adequate)
- Reduce triggers: shadows, curtains, dark rooms, patterned wallpaper, mirrors
- Validate feelings, not the hallucination: 'That sounds frightening — you're safe here, I'm with you'

COGNITIVE FLUCTUATION MANAGEMENT:
- Document periods of lucidity vs. severe confusion with time stamps
- During lucid periods: important conversations, assessments, care planning
- During confused periods: reassurance, simplification, ensure safety
- Educate family: 'The fluctuation is part of the disease — do not assume the worst period is the baseline'
- Educate family: 'Do not make major decisions (e.g., long-term care placement) based solely on the worst day'

RBD SAFETY:
- Lower the bed to the lowest position at night
- Place mattress on the floor or use padded rails
- Remove sharp or hard objects from the bedside
- Consider bed partner sleeping in separate bed if injury risk is high
- Clonazepam 0.5 mg at bedtime (ordered) to reduce acting-out behaviors

AUTONOMIC MANAGEMENT:
- Compression stockings: put on before getting out of bed
- Adequate hydration and sodium intake
- Avoid large, high-carbohydrate meals (postprandial hypotension)
- BP medication review: antihypertensives may need dose reduction

sections.title: Patient and Family Teaching
sections.content:
Critical Family Education Points:

1. The Antipsychotic Warning — MUST TEACH:
'The most important thing I need you to know: if your family member ever needs to go to the emergency room or is admitted to hospital, you MUST tell every doctor, nurse, and paramedic: They have Lewy body dementia. They CANNOT receive haloperidol, olanzapine, risperidone, or most other antipsychotics. These medications can cause a severe reaction that can be life-threatening. Carry a medication alert card with you.'

2. Understanding Cognitive Fluctuation:
'The dramatic changes in your family member's alertness and awareness from day to day — or even hour to hour — are a symptom of the disease, not a sign that they are getting much worse or suddenly recovering. Both extremes are part of Lewy body dementia.'

3. Visual Hallucinations:
'Your family member may describe seeing people or animals that aren't there. These are a hallucination from the disease. In the early stages, they are usually calm and clear — your family member may have good insight that these aren't real. If the hallucinations become frightening, contact the care team. Do not argue or say they're imagining it — this causes distress without helping.'

4. Fall Prevention at Home:
'Your family member has a much higher fall risk than most people with memory problems — because of both the brain changes from Lewy body disease AND the movement problems (parkinsonism). Please: remove rugs, ensure good lighting in all rooms, use grab bars in the bathroom, ensure appropriate footwear at all times.'

5. Sleep Safety for RBD:
'Your family member may physically act out their dreams — punching, kicking, or shouting during sleep. This is called REM sleep behaviour disorder. It is not dangerous intent — they are dreaming. For safety: lower the bed, remove objects from the bedside, and consider separate sleeping arrangements if you have been injured.'

6. Prognosis:
'Lewy body dementia tends to have a more fluctuating and unpredictable course than Alzheimer's disease. Life expectancy after diagnosis is typically 5–8 years, but varies considerably. The rate of decline can be uneven — good periods and difficult periods.'

sections.title: Documentation Requirements
sections.content:
Medication Safety Documentation:
- Document LBD diagnosis prominently in medication administration record, care plan, and allergy/alert section
- Document 'Antipsychotic contraindicated — Lewy body dementia diagnosis' in clinical notes and discharge summaries
- If antipsychotic is ordered against the contraindication: document prescriber contacted, reason given, nurse's concern expressed, outcome of discussion

Fluctuation Documentation:
- Time-stamp all behavioral observations — fluctuation pattern must be documented to distinguish LBD from delirium
- Example: '0800: Patient alert, oriented ×3, joking with staff, scored 24/30 on MoCA. 1400: Patient unarousable except to sternal rub, incoherent when responding. 1700: Patient alert again, eating dinner independently.'

Fall Documentation:
- Circumstances of fall: activity, time, witnesses, immediate response
- Injury assessment with neurological check (if head involved)
- Pre-fall neurological status if known
- Post-fall interventions implemented
- Physician and family notification with time

Hallucination Documentation:
- Frequency, content, patient's emotional response, insight level
- Safety assessment: are hallucinations causing the patient to take dangerous action?
- Interventions implemented and effectiveness

RBD Documentation:
- Frequency, description, injuries, bed partner involvement
- Safety measures in place
- Clonazepam effectiveness if prescribed

sections.title: Clinical Pearls
content: The most lethal nursing error in LBD: administering haloperidol PRN for agitation. In LBD, haloperidol is not a calming agent — it is a trigger for a potentially fatal rigidity syndrome. Always check for LBD diagnosis before administering any antipsychotic.

Cognitive fluctuation in LBD is intrinsic to the disease — it mimics delirium but has no acute medical precipitant. The key question: 'Has the patient been like this episodically for months (LBD fluctuation) or is this a NEW acute change (delirium)?' Both can coexist.

Visual hallucinations in LBD are characteristically detailed and formed (people, animals, scenes) — vague shapes and shadows suggest delirium or medication effect. The calmness with which early LBD patients describe their hallucinations ('I know it isn't real, but there's a small girl in the corner') is pathognomonic.

REM sleep behaviour disorder is the most sensitive prodromal marker of LBD — it may precede dementia by 20+ years. When an older patient presents with a new dementia and their spouse reports 'they've been hitting me in their sleep for years' — think LBD.

Rivastigmine is the only AChEI with an RCT specifically in DLB, showing robust reduction in cognitive fluctuation, attention, and hallucinations — the cholinergic deficit in LBD is more severe than in AD, making the treatment response stronger.

The 1-year rule is a clinical rule, not a biological boundary — neuropathologically, DLB and PDD are the same disease. A patient initially diagnosed with Parkinson's disease who develops dementia >1 year after motor onset is reclassified as PDD, with the SAME antipsychotic contraindication.

mnemonics.name: 1-Year Rule
mnemonics.breakdown: DLB = Dementia FIRST (or within 1 year of motor); PDD = Parkinson's Motor FIRST, then dementia >1 year later

patternRules: Vivid, detailed hallucinations (people, animals) + cognitive fluctuation + parkinsonism = LBD until proven otherwise
RBD history in a patient presenting with dementia = STRONGLY suggests LBD
Antipsychotic + LBD = NEVER (except quetiapine/clozapine in extremis, with consent)
Parkinsonism that responds POORLY to levodopa = think LBD
Motor symptoms <1 year of dementia onset = DLB; motor >1 year before dementia = PDD

sections.title: Safety Alerts
content: 🚨 ANTIPSYCHOTICS ARE POTENTIALLY FATAL IN LBD: This is not a relative contraindication — it is a CRITICAL safety issue. 50% of LBD patients experience severe, potentially fatal reactions (rigidity, coma, hyperthermia, death) with haloperidol, risperidone, olanzapine, and most antipsychotics. NEVER administer these without confirming no LBD diagnosis. Flag LBD diagnosis in every clinical encounter.

🚨 EXTREME FALL RISK: LBD patients have THREE simultaneous fall risk factors: (1) parkinsonism impairing gait and balance; (2) cognitive fluctuation causing sudden periods of impaired judgment; (3) orthostatic hypotension causing position-change syncope. Comprehensive fall prevention is mandatory.

🚨 ASPIRATION RISK: Parkinsonism impairs the oropharyngeal phase of swallowing. Aspiration pneumonia is one of the leading causes of death in LBD. Formal SLP dysphagia assessment is required when any swallowing difficulty is noted. Never rush mealtimes in LBD.

🚨 RBD INJURY RISK: Patients and BED PARTNERS are at injury risk from RBD. Assess specifically for this. The patient who is 'fine in the daytime' may be assaulting their spouse every night while asleep. Environmental safety modifications and clonazepam are essential.

🚨 EMERGENCY DEPARTMENT RISK: In an acute care or ED setting, the greatest risk to an LBD patient is an on-call physician who does not know their diagnosis prescribing haloperidol for 'agitation.' Ensure LBD diagnosis is VISIBLE on the chart, wristband alert or allergy documentation, and that family members carry a medication alert card.

sections.title: Exam Tips
content: The single highest-yield LBD exam point: antipsychotic CONTRAINDICATION. When any question describes a patient with LBD who is agitated and asks what to do — the answer NEVER involves haloperidol, olanzapine, or risperidone. Non-pharmacological management is first; if pharmacological is required, quetiapine is the least dangerous option.

The 1-year rule appears consistently on NCLEX, CNPLE, and FNP exams: dementia before or within 1 year of motor symptoms = DLB; motor symptoms >1 year before dementia = PDD. The antipsychotic contraindication applies to BOTH.

Visual hallucination questions in LBD: features that suggest LBD over delirium — (1) VIVID, FORMED (people, animals, not vague); (2) early in the dementia course; (3) patient often has insight; (4) non-threatening initially; (5) no acute medical precipitant.
RBD is high-yield as a prodromal marker: a question describing a history of 'acting out dreams physically' in a patient who THEN develops dementia is pointing to LBD as the diagnosis.

Rivastigmine in LBD: when a question asks which medication has specific evidence for treating hallucinations and cognitive fluctuation in LBD (not just AD) — the answer is rivastigmine (AChEI with LBD-specific RCT evidence).

Quetiapine vs. haloperidol in LBD: quetiapine is the LEAST dangerous (lowest D2 binding), but STILL carries risk and requires consent. It is 'safer' not 'safe'. This distinction matters for exam answers.
